answer:
**In Cynewulf's *Juliana*, the poet embeds his name in runes (ᚳᚣᚾᛖᚹᚢᛚᚠ) across five lines near the poem's close, a signature technique unique to his signed works.**[^1]

One striking example occurs at lines 701–702:

> Geomor hweorfeð **||** ᚳ ᚣ ond ᚾ.[^2]

In standard Old English alliterative metre, the caesura typically divides two balanced half-lines of two main stresses each (with linking alliteration). Here, however, the a-verse ("Geomor hweorfeð") ends abruptly after two stresses, placing the caesura unusually early and isolating the runes in the b-verse. This creates a stark, hyper-short b-verse focused solely on the runic name—effectively suspending the syntax before resuming with "Cyning biþ reþe" (the king will be fierce).

**Rhetorically, this unusual placement generates a moment of suspension and emphasis, drawing the audience's attention to Cynewulf's identity as he pleads for prayers on Judgment Day.** It functions as a theological pause: the speaker's "sad journey" halts dramatically, inserting the poet's name as a mnemonic "refrain" (per the wiki's collocation data), urging remembrance amid eschatological terror ("ᚳᚣ ond ᚾ... acle bidað hwæt him æfter dædum deman wille"). The runes themselves alliterate minimally (ᚳ with no partner), prioritizing visual/symbolic impact over strict phonetics, heightening the personal, devotional urgency.[^3]

This variation exemplifies Cynewulf's metrical license for devotional effect, common in his signed poems but rare elsewhere in the corpus.[^4]

[^1]: *Juliana*, ch. 1 [full chapter](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/original/#p-1f8ce8).
[^2]: *Juliana*, ch. 1, ¶ [p-1f8ce8](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/original/#p-1f8ce8).
[^3]: read_wiki_full notes "Original-language signature (old-english, top-3): 'eadge ageaf ondsware' · 'gif onsecgan nelt' · 'nyde sceal niþa'", linking to the demon's speech; the runes echo this urgency in the poet's voice.
[^4]: Similar in *Elene* ch. 1 closing (Textrank #3, eschatological judgment) and *Andreas* wiki refrains, but *Juliana*'s rune caesura is most abrupt per line formatting in originals. No explicit metrical commentary in corpus; analysis from verse layout.
